title: Styling Options for React Applications

learningObjectives.text: Explain global css using a new example and justify the result.
Explain css modules using a new example and justify the result.
Explain css-in-js using a new example and justify the result.
Explain utility-first css using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use styling options for react applications without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Complex State, Routing, and Styling** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Global CSS** → **CSS Modules** → **CSS-in-JS** → **Utility-first CSS**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Global CSS
sections.body: Global CSS suits resets, tokens, typography, and truly global utilities. Component-specific selectors should not leak broadly.

sections.title: CSS Modules
sections.body: CSS Modules locally scope class names while retaining standard CSS syntax.

sections.title: CSS-in-JS
sections.body: CSS-in-JS can colocate dynamic styles with components but introduces runtime or build complexity and library conventions.

sections.title: Utility-first CSS
sections.body: Utility-first CSS composes small classes directly in markup and relies on shared design tokens and responsive variants.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: CSS Modules locally scope class names.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Styling Options for React Applications connects Global CSS, CSS Modules, CSS-in-JS.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Styling Options for React Applications in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Global CSS deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.”?

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Styling Options for React Applications?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Global CSS, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Styling Options for React Applications?

choices.label: Use Styling Options for React Applications in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

choices.label: Avoid Styling Options for React Applications completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

choices.label: Use Styling Options for React Applications when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Styling Options for React Applications.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
